"Congressional capacity" means the total institutional resources, infrastructure, compensation, and support systems necessary for Congress to effectively perform its constitutional functions, including but not limited to: member and staff compensation, technology systems, security measures, office facilities, member support infrastructure, and operational resources.

(5) Member Representational Allowance (MRA)

"Member Representational Allowance" or "MRA" means the budget allocated to each member of the House of Representatives for official and representational duties, including staff salaries, office expenses, technology, travel, communications, and constituent services.

(6) Duty Station Allowance

"Duty Station Allowance" means the tax-free housing stipend established pursuant to Section 404(a) to assist members in maintaining a residence in the Washington, DC metropolitan area during congressional service.

(7) Professional Standards

"Professional standards" means the methodologies, practices, and ethical standards used by qualified professionals in compensation analysis, human resources management, survey research, statistical analysis, and related fields, including standards established by professional organizations such as the Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM), WorldatWork, and the American Compensation Association.

(8) Benchmark

"Benchmark" means a reference point or comparative standard used to assess the adequacy of congressional resources, including but not limited to: compensation levels in comparable positions (Executive Schedule, Senior Executive Service, state legislators, private sector executives), resource levels in peer legislatures, technology standards in modern organizations, and professional best practices in organizational management.

(9) Review Period

"Review period" means the period during which Congress may consider a Joint Resolution of Disapproval of a Commission Determination, as specified in Section 301(c), consisting of:

  • 60 calendar days for standard Determinations;
  • 90 calendar days for member compensation Determinations;
  • 30 calendar days for emergency Determinations.

(10) Effective Date

"Effective date" means the date on which a Commission Determination takes legal effect, which is the day following expiration of the review period unless Congress passes a Joint Resolution of Disapproval that is signed by the President or becomes law over Presidential veto.

(11) Phase 1

"Phase 1" means Year 1 following enactment (Months 1-12), during which the Commission is established, develops methodologies, and conducts baseline studies, but issues no binding Determinations, as specified in Section 201(b).

(12) Phase 2

"Phase 2" means Year 2 following enactment (Months 13-24), during which the Commission issues recommendations and analysis regarding technology infrastructure and security matters but does not yet have binding authority, as specified in Section 201(c).

(13) Phase 3

"Phase 3" means Year 3 and beyond (Month 25 onward), during which the Commission has full authority over all domains specified in Title IV including staff compensation, technology, security, member support infrastructure, MRA, and member compensation, as specified in Section 201(d).

(14) Staff

"Staff" means individuals employed by members of Congress or congressional committees to assist with legislative, constituent service, communications, administrative, and other official duties.

(15) Technology Infrastructure

"Technology infrastructure" means the hardware, software, networks, cybersecurity systems, data management tools, communications platforms, and related systems necessary for effective congressional operations.

(16) Security Classification

"Security Classification" means the system for assessing member threat levels and allocating security resources established pursuant to Section 403, including physical security for offices and residences, cybersecurity systems, and protective details as warranted.

(17) Compensation Package

"Compensation package" means the total compensation provided to members of Congress including base salary, retirement/pension benefits, health insurance, life insurance, disability insurance, and other benefits, as determined by the Commission pursuant to Section 406.

(18) Benchmarking

"Benchmarking" means the systematic process of comparing congressional resources to relevant reference points to assess adequacy and competitiveness, using professional methodologies for data collection, analysis, and interpretation.

(19) Regional Cost Differential

"Regional cost differential" means the adjustment to resource allocations that accounts for geographic variation in costs, including office rent, staff salaries, transportation, and other operational expenses, as applied by the Commission pursuant to Section 405(b).

(20) Independent Funding

"Independent funding" means the funding mechanism established pursuant to Section 501(b) whereby the Commission receives operational funding through assessment on MRA appropriations or other means not requiring annual congressional appropriations, thereby insulating Commission operations from appropriations politics.

docs.title: (a) Superseding Provisions
docs.text:
(1) Congressional Compensation Statutes

To the extent this Act conflicts with existing statutes governing congressional compensation, this Act supersedes such statutes:

  1. 2 U.S.C. Â§ 4501 (Congressional Pay Adjustment):
  2. The automatic COLA mechanism in 2 U.S.C. Â§ 4501 is superseded by Commission authority under Section 406;

  3. Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission determines congressional compensation, and any prior statutory formulas do not apply unless the Commission specifically incorporates them into a Determination;

  4. Other Compensation Statutes:

  5. Any other statutes establishing congressional compensation levels or adjustment mechanisms are superseded by this Act to the extent of conflict;
  6. Commission authority under Title IV controls;

(2) MRA Formulas

  1. Statutory MRA Provisions:
  2. Existing statutory formulas for Member Representational Allowance are superseded by Commission authority under Section 405 beginning in Phase 3;

  3. Commission may retain existing formula methodology if it determines the formula remains adequate, or may establish new methodology;

  4. Administrative Regulations:

  5. House and Senate administrative regulations regarding MRA allocation remain in effect except to the extent superseded by Commission Determinations;

(3) Staff Compensation Provisions

  1. Existing Salary Caps:
  2. Existing caps on congressional staff salaries tied to member compensation (e.g., limitations in 2 U.S.C. Â§ 4575) are superseded by Commission authority under Section 401 beginning in Phase 3;

  3. Commission may establish staff salary bands independent of member compensation;

  4. Benefits Provisions:

  5. Existing staff benefits provisions remain in effect except to the extent modified by Commission Determinations;

(4) Control Provision

Where this Act is inconsistent with any prior statute, Executive Order, regulation, or other legal provision, this Act controls to the full extent of any conflict.

docs.title: (b) Preserved Authorities
docs.text:
The following authorities remain intact and are not affected by this Act:

(1) Congressional Rules-Making Authority

  1. Article I, Section 5 Protected:
  2. Each House of Congress retains exclusive authority to determine its own rules of proceedings pursuant to Article I, Section 5 of the Constitution;

  3. Commission has no authority over congressional rules, procedures, or internal governance;

  4. Rules Not Subject to Commission:

  5. Legislative calendars and schedules;
  6. Committee structure and jurisdiction;
  7. Floor procedures and debate rules;
  8. Voting procedures;
  9. Any other matters of internal House or Senate rules;

(2) Ethics Enforcement

  1. Ethics Committees Retain Full Authority:
  2. House Committee on Ethics retains full authority over House ethics matters;
  3. Senate Select Committee on Ethics retains full authority over Senate ethics matters;

  4. Commission has no ethics enforcement authority;

  5. Commission May Make Recommendations:

  6. Commission may recommend modifications to ethics rules that affect member or staff compensation or career sustainability (e.g., spousal employment restrictions per Section 404(c)(4));
  7. Ethics Committees retain sole authority to adopt or reject such recommendations;

(3) Internal Governance

  1. Leadership Selection:
  2. Selection of House Speaker, Majority/Minority Leaders, Whips, and other leadership positions remains exclusively within each chamber's authority;

  3. Commission has no role in leadership selection;

  4. Committee Assignments:

  5. Committee assignments and chairmanships remain exclusively within each chamber's authority;

  6. Commission has no role in committee assignments;

  7. Member Discipline:

  8. Each chamber retains exclusive authority to discipline its members pursuant to Article I, Section 5;
  9. Commission has no disciplinary authority;

(4) Legislative Functions

Commission has no authority over:

  1. Legislative Priorities:
  2. What legislation Congress considers;
  3. Legislative agenda and priorities;

  4. Policy content of legislation;

  5. Voting:

  6. How members vote;
  7. Voting procedures;

  8. Vote outcomes;

  9. Oversight:

  10. Congressional oversight of executive branch;
  11. Investigation authorities;
  12. Subpoena powers (except Commission's own investigative authority);

(5) Constitutional Qualifications

  1. Article I Requirements:
  2. Constitutional qualifications for House and Senate (age, citizenship, residency) remain as specified in Article I, Sections 2 and 3;

  3. Commission has no authority to modify constitutional qualifications;

  4. Judicial Interpretation:

  5. Courts retain authority to interpret constitutional qualification requirements;
  6. Commission bound by judicial interpretation;

docs.title: (c) Coordination with Other Agencies
docs.text:
(1) Office of Personnel Management

Commission coordinates with OPM regarding:

  1. Federal Benefits Programs:
  2. Integration of congressional benefits with federal employee benefits (FEHB, FEGLI, FERS, TSP);

  3. Consistency with federal benefits administration;

  4. Pay Systems:

  5. Coordination with Executive Schedule and SES pay systems;
  6. Use of OPM data for benchmarking;

(2) General Services Administration

Commission coordinates with GSA regarding:

  1. Office Space:
  2. Congressional office space and leasing;

  3. Facilities standards;

  4. Technology Procurement:

  5. Bulk purchasing arrangements;
  6. Federal technology standards;

(3) U.S. Capitol Police

Commission coordinates with Capitol Police regarding:

  1. Security Assessments:
  2. Threat assessments for members (Section 403(a));

  3. Security recommendations and standards;

  4. Protective Services:

  5. Implementation of security measures funded by Commission Determinations;

(4) House and Senate Administrative Offices

Commission coordinates with:

  1. Chief Administrative Officer of the House:
  2. MRA administration;
  3. Technology infrastructure;

  4. Operational implementation of Determinations;

  5. Secretary of the Senate:

  6. Senate resource administration;
  7. Technology infrastructure;
  8. Operational implementation of Determinations;

(5) Congressional Budget Office

Commission coordinates with CBO regarding:

  1. Cost Estimates:
  2. CBO provides cost estimates for Commission Determinations;

  3. Budget scoring and impact analysis;

  4. Economic Data:

  5. CBO provides economic data and projections;
  6. Commission uses CBO data for Determinations;

(6) Government Accountability Office

Commission relationship with GAO specified in Section 601 (annual audits and oversight).

docs.title: (a) General Severability
docs.text: If any provision of this Act, or the application of any provision to any person or circumstance, is held invalid by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remainder of this Act and the application of such provision to other persons or circumstances shall not be affected and shall remain in full force and effect.

docs.title: (b) Title Severability
docs.text:
If any entire Title of this Act is held invalid:

  1. Other Titles Continue:
  2. All other Titles remain in full force and effect;

  3. Commission continues to operate with valid authorities;

  4. Example:

  5. If Title IV, Section 406 (Member Compensation Package) held invalid, Commission retains authority over Sections 401-405 (staff compensation, technology, security, member support, MRA);

docs.title: (c) Section Severability
docs.text:
If any Section within a Title is held invalid:

  1. Other Sections Continue:
  2. Other sections within same Title remain valid;

  3. Commission implements valid provisions;

  4. Example:

  5. If Section 404(a) (Duty Station Allowance) held invalid, other provisions of Section 404 (travel, family support, work-life balance) remain valid;

docs.title: (d) Subsection Severability
docs.text: If any subsection or paragraph is held invalid, other subsections and paragraphs remain valid and enforceable.

docs.title: (e) Commission Continuity
docs.text:
If any Commission authority is invalidated, Commission continues to operate with remaining valid authorities:

  1. Partial Authority:
  2. Commission exercises all authorities not invalidated;

  3. Continues to issue Determinations within valid authority;

  4. Operational Continuity:

  5. Commission remains established and operational;
  6. Funding continues;
  7. Staff and operations continue;

docs.title: (f) Intent
docs.text: It is the intent of Congress that this Act be severable to the maximum extent possible, ensuring that valid provisions remain enforceable even if some provisions are invalidated.

docs.title: (a) Constitutional Supremacy Clause
docs.text: Nothing in this Act shall be construed to supersede, modify, or limit any provision of the Constitution of the United States.

docs.title: (b) Constitutional Conflicts
docs.text:
To the extent any provision of this Act conflicts with the Constitution:

  1. Constitution Prevails:
  2. Constitutional provision controls;

  3. Conflicting statutory provision is invalid to extent of conflict;

  4. Severability Applies:

  5. Invalid provision severed pursuant to Section 703;
  6. Remainder of Act continues in effect;

docs.title: (c) Judicial Interpretation
docs.text:
1. Constitutional Avoidance:
  2. Courts should interpret this Act, where possible, in a manner consistent with the Constitution;

  3. Interpretations avoiding constitutional conflicts preferred;

  4. Constitutional Challenges:

  5. Constitutional challenges to provisions of this Act resolved by courts;
  6. Commission bound by judicial interpretation;

docs.title: (a) Liberal Construction
docs.text:
This Act shall be liberally construed to achieve its purposes of:

  1. Establishing professional, evidence-based determination of congressional compensation and resources;
  2. Removing political vulnerability from congressional capacity decisions;
  3. Ensuring adequate institutional resources for effective congressional operations;
  4. Providing transparency and accountability in resource allocation;
  5. Enabling middle-class Americans to serve in Congress without financial hardship.

docs.title: (b) No Inference of Authority
docs.text:
Nothing in this Act shall be construed as:

  1. Granting Congress New Constitutional Authority:
  2. This Act does not expand Congress's constitutional powers;

  3. Congress exercises only authorities granted by the Constitution;

  4. Limiting Congressional Authority:

  5. This Act does not diminish Congress's constitutional authorities;

  6. Congress retains all powers granted by the Constitution;

  7. Establishing New Constitutional Requirements:

  8. This Act is statutory, not constitutional;
  9. Can be amended by future Congresses through regular legislation;

docs.title: (c) State Authority Preserved
docs.text:
Except where expressly preempted by this Act, states retain authority over:

  1. State Legislator Compensation:
  2. States determine their own legislator compensation;

  3. This Act does not govern state legislature compensation;

  4. State Employee Benefits:

  5. States determine benefits for state employees;

  6. This Act applies only to federal members of Congress;

  7. Other State Matters:

  8. All other matters within state jurisdiction remain with states;
  9. This Act is limited to federal congressional capacity;

docs.title: (d) No Private Right of Action Except as Specified
docs.text:
This Act does not create private right of action except:

  1. Judicial Review Provisions:
  2. Judicial review of Determinations as specified in Section 603;

  3. No broader right of action created;

  4. Standing Requirements:

  5. Party must have standing under Article III to challenge Determination;
  6. Generalized grievance insufficient for standing;

docs.title: Section 706. Effective Date and Temporal Definitions
(a) General Effective Date
docs.text: Except as otherwise specifically provided in this Act, this Act takes effect on the date of enactment.

docs.title: (b) Commission Establishment
docs.text:
Commission establishment begins immediately upon enactment:

  1. Appointment Process:
  2. Appointing authorities begin appointment process immediately;

  3. Goal: All appointments completed within 6 months of enactment;

  4. First Meeting:

  5. Commission holds first meeting within 60 days of final member appointment or 7 months after enactment, whichever is earlier;
  6. First meeting includes term staggering determination and Chair election;

docs.title: (c) Phased Implementation
docs.text:
Authority phases as specified in Title II:

  1. Phase 1 (Year 1, Months 1-12):
  2. Begins on date of enactment;
  3. Commission establishment, methodology development, baseline studies;

  4. No binding Determinations;

  5. Phase 2 (Year 2, Months 13-24):

  6. Begins first day of Month 13 following enactment;
  7. Limited technical authority (recommendations only);

  8. Technology and security analysis;

  9. Phase 3 (Year 3+, Month 25 onward):

  10. Begins first day of Month 25 following enactment;
  11. Full authority over all domains in Title IV;
  12. Binding Determinations issued;

docs.title: (d) Fiscal Year Alignment
docs.text:
1. Budget Year:

  2. Commission operates on federal fiscal year (October 1 - September 30);

  3. First Partial Fiscal Year:

  4. If enacted mid-fiscal year, Commission operates partial fiscal year until next October 1;

docs.title: (e) Determination Effective Dates
docs.text:
Individual Determinations specify their own effective dates:

  1. Default:

  2. Effective date is day following review period expiration unless otherwise specified;

  3. Delayed Implementation:

  4. Determination may specify future effective date for implementation;

  5. Example: "This Determination takes effect October 1, [year], at the start of the next fiscal year";

  6. Retroactive Effect Prohibited:

  7. Determinations may not have retroactive effect except for technical corrections;

docs.title: (a) Current Resource Allocations Continue
docs.text:
Until Commission issues first Determinations in Phase 3:

  1. Current Compensation:
  2. Member compensation continues at levels in effect immediately before Phase 3;

  3. COLA mechanisms (if not blocked) continue unless superseded;

  4. Current MRA:

  5. MRA continues at levels and formulas in effect;

  6. House Administration determines MRA per existing procedures;

  7. Current Staff Compensation:

  8. Staff salary caps and provisions continue;

  9. No changes until Commission Determinations;

  10. Current Benefits:

  11. All existing benefits continue unchanged;

docs.title: (b) No Reduction in Existing Resources
docs.text:
1. Protection Against Cuts:
  2. No member or staff member receives salary reduction due to this Act;
  3. No office receives MRA reduction due to this Act;

  4. Transition is additive or neutral, never subtractive;

  5. Grandfather Provisions:

  6. Current compensation and benefits grandfathered;
  7. Commission may increase but not decrease resources during transition;

docs.title: (c) Existing Staff Protections
docs.text:
1. No Adverse Employment Actions:
  2. No staff member terminated or demoted due to this Act;

  3. Staff positions protected during transition;

  4. Current Staff Rights:

  5. All existing staff employment rights continue;
  6. Union contracts (if any) remain in effect;

docs.title: (d) Coordination with House and Senate Administrative Offices
docs.text:
Commission works with administrative offices to ensure smooth transition:

  1. Implementation Planning:
  2. Joint planning for Determination implementation;
  3. Operational coordination;

  4. System integration;

  5. Technical Assistance:

  6. Administrative offices provide Commission with operational data;

  7. Commission provides administrative offices with implementation guidance;

  8. Payroll and Benefits Administration:

  9. Administrative offices implement compensation Determinations through payroll systems;
  10. Benefits administration coordinated;

docs.title: (e) Existing Contracts and Obligations
docs.text:
1. Contract Continuity:
  2. Existing contracts for technology, security, facilities remain in effect;

  3. Commission Determinations may modify future contracts but do not invalidate existing ones;

  4. Obligation Continuity:

  5. Existing financial obligations continue;
  6. Commission considers existing obligations when making Determinations;

docs.title: (a) Amendments to Title 2, United States Code
docs.text:
1. 2 U.S.C. Â§ 4501 (Congressional Pay Adjustment):

At the end of section 4501, add the following:

"(d) SUPERSEDING AUTHORITY. -- Beginning on the date the Congressional Capacity Commission established by the Congressional Capacity Commission Act first issues a Determination regarding congressional compensation pursuant to Phase 3 authority under that Act, the provisions of this section are superseded by Commission authority to the extent of any conflict. The Commission may incorporate the automatic adjustment mechanism in this section into its Determinations if it determines such mechanism remains appropriate."

  1. Other Amendments as Necessary:

The Director of the Office of the Law Revision Counsel shall make such technical and conforming amendments to Title 2 as necessary to reflect the authorities established by this Act.

docs.title: (b) Coordination with Ethics Rules
docs.text:
1. House Ethics Rules:

The House Committee on Ethics shall review existing ethics rules to identify any rules that may require modification to implement Commission Determinations, particularly regarding: - Spousal employment restrictions (Section 404(c)(4)); - Outside income limitations; - Gift rules;

The Committee shall report findings and recommendations to the House within 180 days of the first Commission Determination in Phase 3.

  1. Senate Ethics Rules:

The Senate Select Committee on Ethics shall conduct parallel review and report to the Senate within the same timeframe.

docs.title: (c) No Automatic Repeal
docs.text:
1. Existing Laws Remain Unless Superseded:
  2. Existing laws governing congressional compensation and resources remain in effect unless specifically superseded by Commission Determinations;

  3. Commission may choose to retain existing statutory provisions if they remain adequate;

  4. Commission Discretion:

  5. Commission determines which existing provisions to retain, modify, or replace;
  6. Determinations specify relationship to existing law;

docs.title: (a) Initial Report to Congress
docs.text:
Within 180 days of the Commission's first meeting, the Commission shall submit a report to Congress containing:

  1. Work Plan:
  2. Detailed Phase 1 work plan;
  3. Timeline for methodology development;
  4. Plans for baseline studies;

  5. Stakeholder engagement strategy;

  6. Budget:

  7. Proposed budget for Year 1 operations;

  8. Explanation of resource needs;

  9. Staffing Plan:

  10. Proposed staffing structure;

  11. Key positions and recruitment timeline;

  12. Methodology Framework:

  13. Preliminary framework for compensation benchmarking;
  14. Data sources to be used;
  15. Professional standards to be followed;

docs.title: (b) Phase 2 Report
docs.text:
At the beginning of Phase 2, Commission submits report containing:

  1. Phase 1 Accomplishments:
  2. Methodology development completed;
  3. Baseline studies completed;

  4. Lessons learned;

  5. Phase 2 Plans:

  6. Technology and security analysis plans;

  7. Timeline for Phase 2 activities;

  8. Phase 3 Preparation:

  9. Plans for transitioning to binding authority;
  10. Anticipated early Determinations;

docs.title: (c) Ongoing Congressional Briefings
docs.text:
Commission provides regular briefings to congressional committees:

  1. Quarterly Briefings:
  2. Briefings every quarter during Phase 1 and Phase 2;

  3. Semi-annual briefings during Phase 3;

  4. Major Decision Briefings:

  5. Briefings before issuing major Determinations;

  6. Opportunity for committee feedback;

  7. Ad Hoc Briefings:

  8. Upon committee request;
  9. Addressing specific concerns or questions;

docs.title: (a) Ten-Year Review
docs.text:
Ten years after the first Commission Determination in Phase 3, GAO shall conduct comprehensive review of Commission effectiveness:

  1. Review Scope:
  2. Assessment of Commission performance;
  3. Evaluation of whether Commission achieved statutory purposes;
  4. Analysis of congressional capacity improvements;

  5. Recommendations for statutory amendments;

  6. Report to Congress:

  7. GAO submits review report to Congress;
  8. Congress considers whether amendments needed;

docs.title: (b) No Automatic Sunset
docs.text: This Act does not sunset automatically; Commission continues operations indefinitely unless Congress amends this Act.

docs.title: (c) Congressional Authority to Amend
docs.text: Congress retains authority to amend this Act at any time through regular legislative process.

docs.title: Section 711. Short Title Usage
docs.text:
This Act may be cited as:

  1. "Congressional Capacity Commission Act";
  2. "CCC Act"; or
  3. "Commission Act of [Year of Enactment]"

docs.title: Section 712. Congressional Findings and Purpose (Cross-Reference)
docs.text: The findings and purposes of this Act are set forth in Section 2 (Findings) and are incorporated here by reference.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text:
Revision 1.1 (Current)

  • Reformatted to comply with APAI Document Production Manual standards
  • Removed duplicate header structure and table of contents
  • Standardized footer format with revision history

Prepared by Albert Ramos for The American Policy Architecture Institute

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Published January 2026

Modernizing Congressional Operations and Electoral Administration

docs.title: The Institutional Challenge
docs.text:
Congress operates with constitutional machinery designed for 4 million Americans now serving 335 million -- an 84-fold population increase with no corresponding institutional adaptation. Representatives serve 760,000 constituents each, twenty-five times the founding ratio and six to seven times larger than peer democracies. States maintain fifty different ballot access regimes creating litigation risk and administrative burden. Winner-take-all single-member districts enable geometry-driven representation distortions regardless of which party controls redistricting.

These aren't moral failures -- they're operational deficiencies in constitutional infrastructure that reduce congressional capacity, increase dysfunction risk, and create unnecessary vulnerability to demographic and political shocks.

docs.title: The Solution: Simultaneous Integrated Reform
docs.text:
CMA updates electoral and legislative infrastructure through an integrated package of evidence-based reforms. Members receive immediate capacity improvements upon enactment. Electoral reforms launch together at the First CMA Election as a unified system -- producing "the new Congress" rather than "more of the old Congress." Cube root compliance is achieved within approximately 14 years -- three presidential terms.

The core insight: Expanding the House without reforming the electoral system simply creates more gerrymandered districts and amplifies existing dysfunction. CMA ensures STAR voting, multi-member districts, and House expansion debut together as an integrated operating system for American democracy.

docs.title: Tier 1: Immediate Capacity Enhancements (upon enactment)
docs.text:
• Phased compensation increases and automatic COLA adjustments
  • Professional development resources (5% of salary annually)
  • Enhanced staff budgets reducing talent drain to executive agencies
  • Improved pension structures reducing post-Congress lobbying dependency
  • Streamlined Senate appointment authority preventing administrative paralysis

docs.title: Tier 2: Integrated Electoral Reform Launch (First CMA Election)
docs.text:
• Standardized ballot access reducing state-by-state litigation
  • Federal party recognition with Joint Endorsement Lists
  • House expansion to 510 members (first step toward cube root)
  • Multi-member districts with STAR-PR begin phasing in (25%)
  • STAR voting implemented for all federal elections

docs.title: Tier 3: Competency Transparency (Second CMA Election)
docs.text:
• FCAO examinations become operational
  • Scores appear on ballots for voter information
  • Non-qualifying structure preserved -- no minimum score required

docs.title: 1. Immediate Benefits, Integrated Reform
docs.text:
All compensation and capacity improvements take effect immediately upon enactment. Electoral changes launch together at the First CMA Election as a unified system -- not piecemeal over decades.

Current members receive full benefits regardless of when they face new electoral systems. The structure doesn't ask you to vote for distant uncertainty -- it provides present certainty with near-term modernization that members who vote for CMA will see during their careers.

docs.title: 2. Risk Mitigation Through Standardization
docs.text:
Ballot Access Standardization (Title I)

  • Eliminates fifty-state patchwork creating litigation exposure
  • Reduces administrative burden on state election officials
  • Provides predictable framework preventing ballot access disputes
  • 0.5% signature threshold (capped at 5,000 statewide) balances accessibility with legitimacy

Federal Party Recognition Standards (Title II)

  • Establishes clear criteria preventing state-by-state arbitrary decisions
  • Reduces litigation over party status disputes
  • Provides transparency through Joint Endorsement Lists showing coalition structures

docs.title: 3. Institutional Capacity Restoration
docs.text:
House Expansion (Title III)

  • Initial expansion to 510 members at First CMA Election
  • Biennial +35 seat increases until cube root compliance (~720 members)
  • Cube root achieved within ~14 years -- three presidential terms
  • No member loses their seat -- expansion only, no displacement
  • Reduces constituent load from 760,000:1 to approximately 465,000:1
  • Improves district staff capacity for constituent services
  • Dilutes individual member obstruction power toward founders' design
  • Less expensive than expanding executive branch capacity equivalently

Enhanced Compensation (Title VI)

  • Phased salary increases over five years:
  • House: $174,000 (current) rising to $250,000 (Year 5)
  • Senate: $174,000 (current) rising to $300,000 (Year 5)
  • The Senate differential reflects distinct constitutional responsibilities (advice and consent, statewide constituencies, six-year terms)
  • Automatic COLA tied to Employment Cost Index begins after Year 5
  • Enhanced pension accrual (2.5% vs. 1.7%)
  • Professional development allowance (5% of base salary: $12,500 House / $15,000 Senate)
  • Improved staff budgets through enhanced MRA (35% increase upon initial expansion)

These aren't "raises" -- they're institutional capability improvements reducing dependence on fundraising, post-Congress lobbying, and executive branch salary competition.

docs.title: 4. American-Developed Voting Methods
docs.text:
STAR Voting (Score Then Automatic Runoff)

  • American innovation, not imported foreign model
  • Eliminates vote-splitting without complex elimination rounds
  • Administratively simpler than ranked-choice voting systems
  • Ensures winners have both high overall support AND majority preference
  • Reduces manipulation opportunities regardless of which party controls process
  • Consistent voter experience across all federal elections

Unified Election Structure

  • Single general elections for all federal offices -- no state-administered primaries
  • All qualified candidates appear directly on November ballot
  • STAR voting ensures winners have broad support without preliminary filtering
  • Significant cost savings: one election instead of two
  • Higher turnout (general elections consistently outperform primaries)
  • Consistent voter experience regardless of district type or state of residence
  • Voters choose individual candidates, not party lists
  • Party autonomy preserved: parties may conduct internal nomination processes at their own expense

Multi-Member Districts with Proportional Representation

  • Mathematically eliminates geometry-driven representation distortions
  • Makes redistricting manipulation ineffective for any party
  • Historically American (used 1789-1967) -- reinstating proven method
  • Phases in gradually: 25% to 50% to 75% to 90% to 100% over five election cycles

Administrative Efficiency Gains:

  • States save millions annually on duplicate primary infrastructure
  • Voters make one trip to polls for federal elections instead of two
  • Election administrators handle one federal election cycle instead of two

docs.title: 5. Competency Transparency Without Barriers
docs.text:
FCAO Examinations (Title V)

  • Non-qualifying information mechanism -- no minimum score required
  • Candidates scoring 0% can still run and serve if voters choose them
  • Voters remain the sole gatekeepers -- FCAO provides data, not authority
  • Multi-branch appointment structure (President, Chief Justice, House Speaker, Senate Leader) prevents partisan capture
  • Zero power to block any candidate from ballot or service
  • Comparable to nutritional labels -- information for consumer choice, not regulatory barrier
  • Launches at Second CMA Election with adequate development runway

docs.title: Constitutional Authority
docs.text:
Elections Clause (Article I, Section 4) provides plenary congressional power over federal election administration. Supreme Court precedent confirms authority over:

  • Voting methods (Arizona State Legislature v. Arizona Independent Redistricting Commission)
  • Ballot access standards (U.S. Term Limits, Inc. v. Thornton)
  • Manner of holding elections (Ex parte Yarbrough)

House Size Adjustment requires only ordinary legislation -- no constitutional amendment. Congress adjusted House size 13 times between 1789 and 1913. The 1929 cap was statutory choice, not constitutional mandate.

Multi-Member Districts have 178 years of American precedent and explicit Supreme Court validation (Whitcomb v. Chavis, White v. Regester).

docs.title: Tiered Effective Dates
docs.text:
Tier 1 -- Immediate upon enactment:

  • Compensation and capacity improvements (Title VI)
  • Enforcement authority (Title VII)
  • Administrative framework (Title VIII)

Tier 2 -- First CMA Election (second general election after enactment, 18-month minimum):

  • Ballot access standards (Title I)
  • Party recognition framework (Title II)
  • House expansion to 510 (Title III)
  • Multi-member districts begin at 25% (Title IV)
  • STAR voting for all federal elections

Tier 3 -- Second CMA Election (first election after First CMA Election):

  • FCAO examinations operational (Title V)

Ongoing -- Biennial increases:

  • +35 seats per Congress until cube root compliance (~14 years total)

docs.title: Multiple Checkpoints
docs.text: Each expansion provides assessment opportunity before proceeding. If early implementation reveals problems, adjustments occur before further expansion.

docs.title: Severability Protection
docs.text: If courts strike down any provision, others continue operating. Compensation provisions that take effect remain protected even if electoral reforms are challenged.

docs.title: Supermajority Repeal Protection
docs.text: Once compensation mechanisms take effect, reducing or eliminating them requires three-fifths vote in both chambers -- preventing simple-majority backlash.

docs.title: Proven Methods, American Context
docs.text:
These reforms draw on:

Historical American Precedent: Multi-member districts used 1789-1967; Congress adjusted House size 13 times through 1913

Constitutional Authority: Elections Clause grants plenary power; Supreme Court precedent validates multi-member districts (Whitcomb v. Chavis, White v. Regester)

Operational Testing: Pilot implementations in select states provide proof-of-concept before nationwide expansion

The question isn't whether these methods work -- American history and constitutional precedent demonstrate they do. The question is whether congressional infrastructure should continue operating with machinery that hasn't been updated since population tripled.

docs.title: What This Is Not
docs.text:
Not a power grab by either party: Universal benefits regardless of party affiliation, district safety, or seniority. Both caucuses gain capacity improvements. Geometry-proof districts protect both parties from manipulation.

Not deferred to distant future: ~14-year implementation means members who vote for CMA see results during their careers. Voters experience tangible improvements within a political generation. No more 25-year promises that outlive political accountability.

Not swamp expansion: House growth is cheaper than equivalent executive branch capacity expansion. Dilutes rather than concentrates individual member power. Restores constitutional representation ratios without increasing leadership authority.

Not untested innovation: Every reform draws on American historical precedent (multi-member districts 1789-1967; Congress adjusted House size 13 times through 1913).

Not constitutional overreach: Operates within clear Elections Clause authority and congressional house-sizing powers validated by Supreme Court precedent.

Not piecemeal tinkering: Comprehensive operational modernization with integrated reforms launching together -- producing "the new Congress" rather than amplifying existing dysfunction.

docs.title: Bottom Line
docs.text:
CMA modernizes congressional operations and electoral administration using methods with proven track records in American history and tested in U.S. jurisdictions. It provides immediate capacity improvements through enhanced compensation and resources while launching integrated electoral reforms at the First CMA Election.

Reform on a human timescale: Cube root compliance within ~14 years -- three presidential terms. Members who vote for CMA will see results. Voters will experience tangible improvements in representation quality.

Simultaneous integrated launch: STAR voting, multi-member districts, and House expansion debut together, ensuring the new seats are filled through the new system -- not replicated dysfunction.

Immediate member benefits: Compensation and capacity improvements take effect upon enactment, honoring the Grand Bargain that makes passage politically viable.

Current constitutional machinery was designed for 4 million Americans. We now have 335 million. The question isn't whether to modernize -- it's whether to modernize systematically with tested methods and extensive safeguards, or continue with ad-hoc patches that don't address root operational deficiencies.

CMA chooses systematic modernization with:

  • Immediate capacity improvements for all members
  • Simultaneous integrated reform launch at First CMA Election
  • Reform timeline delivering results within a political generation
  • Constitutional defensibility under Elections Clause authority
  • American historical precedent rather than imported models

Prepared by: Albert E. Ramos, Director The American Policy Architecture Institute

Contact: info@policyarchitecture.org Website:

For strategic interpretation and adaptation guidance, see the primary document: CMA House Member Guide.

docs.title: CMA House Member Guide
docs.text: Published January 2026

docs.title: The Opportunity and the Honest Reality
docs.text:
The Congressional Modernization Act represents a once-in-a-generation opportunity to put the Republic on solid institutional footing for the next 250 years. It also changes the electoral landscape in ways that will reward some skills and diminish others.

This document is not a sales pitch. It will not tell you that CMA protects your seat or guarantees your reelection. Some members will thrive under the new system. Others will not. The ones who adapt will have significant advantages. The ones who coast on structural advantages that no longer exist will struggle.

That is how democratic reform is supposed to work.

What this document offers instead is honest guidance: what actually changes, when it changes, what skills transfer, what skills you need to develop, and how to navigate the transition. Think of it as a player's guide to the new Congress.

docs.title: The Tiered Effective Date Structure
docs.text:
CMA uses a tiered triggering system that phases in different provisions at different times:

Tier What Takes Effect When Tier 1 Title VI (compensation, professional development), Title VII (enforcement), Title VIII (general provisions) Upon enactment Tier 2 Titles I-IV (ballot access, party recognition, House expansion, electoral reform) First CMA Election Tier 3 Title V (FCAO examinations) Second CMA Election

The First CMA Election is defined as the second regularly scheduled general election after enactment, with an 18-month minimum runway. If CMA passes in early 2026, the First CMA Election would be November 2028.

docs.title: The House Expansion Schedule
docs.text:
CMA uses accelerated expansion rather than a 25-year phased approach:

Election House Size Change Current 435 -- First CMA Election (~2028) 510 +75 ~2030 545 +35 ~2032 580 +35 ~2034 615 +35 ~2036 650 +35 ~2038 685 +35 ~2040 720 +35 (cube root compliance)

Cube root compliance is reached in approximately 14 years -- three presidential terms. Members who vote for CMA will see results during their careers.

docs.title: Electoral System Changes at the First CMA Election
docs.text:
Everything launches together as an integrated system:

STAR Voting: Score Then Automatic Runoff replaces plurality voting in all federal elections. Voters score candidates 0-5; the two highest-scoring candidates advance to an automatic runoff determined by preference.

Unified General Election Structure: All federal elections use single general elections. No state-administered primaries. Qualified candidates appear directly on the November ballot with party endorsements and (starting at Second CMA Election) FCAO scores displayed. This applies uniformly to House, Senate, and Presidential elections.

Multi-Member Districts: Initial rollout begins at the First CMA Election. States with sufficient population begin transitioning to 3-7 seat districts using STAR-PR (proportional representation).

Joint Endorsement Lists: Candidates may display up to three party endorsements on the ballot, making coalition structures transparent to voters.

docs.title: FCAO Examinations at the Second CMA Election
docs.text:
The Federal Candidate Assessment Office begins administering examinations at the Second CMA Election (approximately 2030). Key features:

  • Non-qualifying: No minimum score required to run or serve
  • Unlimited retakes with only highest score reported
  • Year-round availability at nationwide testing centers
  • Separate House and Senate examinations
  • Scores appear on ballot as voter information, not gatekeeping

docs.title: A Tale of Two Representatives: The Martinez Scenarios
docs.text:
Consider Representative Martinez, who currently holds a competitive district with approximately 30% fervent base support and wins general elections with 51-52% margins.

Martinez faces a choice: adapt to the new system or try to maintain familiar patterns.

docs.title: Scenario A: Martinez Coasts
docs.text:
Decision: "I'll keep doing what I've always done. These reforms will shake out somehow."

First CMA Election (~2028): Martinez's district transitions to multi-member representation as part of a five-seat regional district. Martinez continues the old campaign playbook -- triangulating between base and swing voters, focusing on the same donor networks, treating the election like a single-member race.

The problem: in a five-seat district, Martinez doesn't need 51% anymore. Martinez needs approximately 16-20% to secure one seat. But Martinez hasn't built the kind of durable base loyalty that proportional systems reward. The 30% "fervent base" was never cultivated as a base -- it was taken for granted while Martinez chased swing voters.

Other candidates -- including a newcomer with strong grassroots energy -- have spent the transition period building genuine coalition support. They understand that proportional representation rewards depth of support, not breadth of lukewarm acceptance.

Martinez wins a seat with 22% support in the first multi-member election, but it's closer than expected. The warning signs are there.

2030-2034: Martinez continues the old approach. FCAO scores appear on ballots starting in 2030. Martinez took the exam once, scored 71%, and hasn't retaken it. A challenger who spent two years studying scores 84%.

The coalition dynamics of multi-member districts continue to evolve. Candidates who built reciprocal endorsement networks and genuine base relationships outperform those who didn't. Martinez's support slowly erodes from 22% to 19% to 17%.

2036: A new candidate emerges who has done the adaptation work Martinez skipped. Strong FCAO score. Deep base relationships. Coalition endorsements from allied candidates. Genuine constituent service reputation built through enhanced MRA resources.

Martinez's 17% support falls below the effective threshold. After 20 years in Congress, Martinez loses -- not to a scandal or a wave election, but to a candidate who simply adapted better to the new system.

Career outcome: Martinez served during a historic transition but failed to evolve with it. The skills that won elections under the old system -- swing voter triangulation, donor network maintenance, cautious positioning -- were the wrong skills for the new system.

docs.title: Scenario B: Martinez Adapts
docs.text:
Decision: "The rules are changing. I need to develop new skills."

Transition Period (2026-2028): Before the First CMA Election, Martinez begins the adaptation work:

Base cultivation: Instead of triangulating toward swing voters, Martinez focuses on deepening relationships with the 30% base. What do they actually care about? What constituent services matter most to them? Martinez begins treating them as a durable coalition to cultivate rather than a foundation to take for granted.

Coalition building: Martinez reaches out to candidates who will share the multi-member district. Which ones have compatible but not identical bases? Can they build reciprocal endorsement relationships? Martinez attends to the coalition dynamics that proportional systems reward.

FCAO preparation: Martinez takes the examination early -- in 2027, well before scores appear on ballots. First attempt: 68%. Martinez uses congressional resources and institutional knowledge to study systematically. Second attempt in early 2028: 77%. Third attempt later in 2028: 85%. By the time FCAO scores appear on ballots in 2030, Martinez displays a strong score built through genuine competency development.

Enhanced capacity: Martinez uses the immediate Title VI benefits -- professional development resources, enhanced MRA once multi-member districts activate -- to build better constituent services. More staff. Better casework. Genuine responsiveness that builds the kind of loyalty proportional systems reward.

First CMA Election (~2028): Martinez enters the five-seat district election with preparation complete. The 30% base has been cultivated into genuine 28% durable support. Coalition relationships with allied candidates create reciprocal endorsement benefits. FCAO preparation is underway.

Martinez wins a seat with 28% support -- well above the ~16-20% threshold.

2030-2040: Martinez continues building. FCAO score improves to 89% by 2032. Base relationships deepen. Coalition partnerships mature. Enhanced MRA resources translate into constituent service infrastructure that competitors can't match.

By 2036, Martinez is one of the most effective members in the multi-member district -- not because of structural protection, but because of genuine adaptation and skill development. Martinez retires in 2042 after 26 years in Congress, having successfully navigated the transition.

Career outcome: Martinez recognized that the rules were changing and invested in developing the skills the new system rewards. The same person with the same starting position produced dramatically different outcomes based on adaptation choices.

docs.title: What Matters Less, What Matters More
What Matters Less
docs.text:
Safe district boundaries: Gerrymandering becomes mathematically ineffective under proportional representation. Districts can still be drawn, but the winner-take-all dynamic that makes gerrymandering powerful disappears.

Primary vulnerability: No primaries exist under the unified general election structure. The threat of being "primaried" from your flank disappears entirely.

Swing voter triangulation: You don't need 50%+1 anymore. Chasing the median voter while neglecting your base becomes counterproductive.

Geographic lock-in: Safe districts based on geographic sorting matter less when districts are larger and multi-member. The structural advantages of favorable district lines diminish.

docs.title: What Matters More
docs.text:
Base cultivation: Proportional representation rewards depth of support. A genuine 20% who strongly support you beats a lukewarm 45% who merely prefer you to the alternative.

Coalition relationships: Multi-member districts create opportunities for reciprocal endorsements, shared campaign infrastructure, and coalition building. Members who invest in these relationships will outperform those who don't.

Demonstrated competence: FCAO scores appear on ballots. Members who take preparation seriously will display that competence publicly. Those who don't will display that choice publicly as well.

Authentic representation: Proportional representation lets you represent your actual ideological constituency. You don't need 50%+1; you need your base. Strong positions that energize your coalition are more valuable than cautious positions that offend no one.

What to develop: Figure out what you actually believe. Represent it authentically. Your base will reward conviction; swing voters are no longer the margin of victory.

docs.title: Upon Enactment (~2026)
docs.text:
What happens:

  • Title VI takes effect immediately: phased compensation increases, professional development funding (5% of salary), enhanced pension accrual
  • Anti-retaliation protections activate
  • Administrative preparation begins for Titles I-IV

Your decisions:

  • How to message the compensation increases to constituents
  • How to use professional development resources
  • Whether to begin FCAO preparation early (recommended)
  • Whether to begin base cultivation and coalition building work

docs.title: Pre-First CMA Election (~2026-2028)
docs.text:
What happens:

  • States prepare for new electoral systems
  • House expansion planning proceeds
  • FCAO develops examinations (not yet administered)

Your decisions:

  • Take FCAO examination for practice/baseline (scores won't appear on ballots until Second CMA Election)
  • Build coalition relationships with likely district partners
  • Deepen base relationships
  • Prepare campaign infrastructure for multi-member dynamics

docs.title: First CMA Election (~2028)
docs.text:
What happens:

  • House expands to 510 seats
  • STAR voting implemented for all federal elections
  • Multi-member districts begin rolling out
  • Joint Endorsement Lists appear on ballots
  • No primaries in multi-member districts

Your decisions:

  • Full engagement with new electoral mechanics
  • Coalition endorsement strategies
  • Base mobilization approach
  • Adaptation of campaign messaging for STAR and proportional dynamics

docs.title: Second CMA Election (~2030)
docs.text:
What happens:

  • FCAO scores appear on ballots for first time
  • Multi-member district expansion continues
  • House reaches 545 seats

Your decisions:

  • FCAO score optimization (retake if needed before ballots printed)
  • Continued coalition and base development
  • Leverage enhanced MRA for constituent service advantages

docs.title: Ongoing (~2030-2042)
docs.text:
What happens:

  • Biennial +35 seat expansions continue
  • Multi-member district coverage expands
  • System stabilizes at cube root compliance (~2040-2042)

Your decisions:

  • Continued adaptation and skill development
  • Career planning in context of new system
  • Whether to seek leadership positions in reformed Congress

docs.title: The Honest Bottom Line
docs.text:
CMA changes the game. Some members will thrive. Others will not.

The members who thrive will be those who:

  • Cultivate durable base support rather than chasing swing voters
  • Build coalition relationships with natural allies
  • Take FCAO preparation seriously as genuine competency development
  • Use enhanced resources to provide excellent constituent service
  • Represent their constituencies authentically rather than triangulating constantly

The members who struggle will be those who:

  • Rely on structural advantages that no longer exist
  • Continue old campaign playbooks in a new electoral environment
  • Treat FCAO as a nuisance rather than an opportunity
  • Take their base for granted while chasing voters who no longer determine outcomes
  • Fail to build the coalition relationships proportional systems reward

This is not a threat. It is an honest description of how democratic reform works. The new system rewards different skills than the old system. Those skills are learnable. The question is whether you invest in learning them.

The Republic benefits either way. CMA creates a Congress that better represents the American people, with members who have demonstrated competency, built genuine coalitions, and earned durable support from constituents who chose them with full information.

If you adapt, you have significant advantages: name recognition, institutional knowledge, existing relationships, constituent service infrastructure, and years to prepare while challengers build from scratch.

If you don't adapt, you will be replaced by someone who did.

That is how it should be.

docs.title: Appendix: FCAO as Incumbent Advantage
docs.text: Critics sometimes portray FCAO examinations as threatening to incumbents. The opposite is true -- but only if you take preparation seriously.

docs.title: The Structural Advantages You Have
docs.text:
Time: You can take the exam in Year 1 of your current term, retake it multiple times over years, and display your highest score by your next election. Challengers must prepare during compressed campaign timelines.

Staff support: You have paid staff who can help organize study materials, research constitutional questions, and manage your preparation. Most challengers don't.

Institutional knowledge: You understand congressional procedure, constitutional structure, and legislative process from actually doing the job. The FCAO examination tests exactly this knowledge. You live the material; challengers must study it academically.

Unlimited retakes: Only your highest score ever appears. Early attempts are practice runs. Challengers face one-shot pressure during campaigns.

Lower stakes per attempt: You can take the exam "just to see" without campaign pressure. Each retake is a free option to improve your public credential.

docs.title: The Strategy
docs.text:
• Year 1: Take exam to establish baseline. Score doesn't matter yet.
  • Years 1-2: Study weak areas systematically using congressional resources.
  • Year 2: Retake exam. Improve score.
  • Year 3: Retake again if needed. Optimize.
  • Year 4: Display highest score on reelection ballot.

A challenger who declares candidacy 12 months before the election must study while fundraising, organizing, and campaigning. They take the exam under time pressure and display whatever score they achieve.

You've had years to optimize. The asymmetry is substantial -- but only if you actually use it.

docs.title: The Deeper Point
docs.text:
FCAO preparation makes you better at your job. The examination covers material every legislator should understand: constitutional structure, legislative procedure, policy fundamentals, oversight responsibilities.

Treating FCAO as genuine competency development rather than a test to game produces two benefits: a higher score, and improved effectiveness as a legislator. The latter builds the reputation that earns votes regardless of what appears on the ballot.

docs.title: Immediate Benefits (Upon Enactment)
docs.text: Provision House Senate Notes Year 1 Salary $189,200 $199,200 8.7% increase from current Year 5 Salary $250,000 $300,000 Phased increases Professional Development $12,500/year $15,000/year 5% of salary Pension Accrual 2.5% 2.5% Up from 1.7% COLA Automatic Automatic Tied to Employment Cost Index

docs.title: Multi-Member District Benefits (First CMA Election+)
docs.text: Provision Amount Notes Enhanced MRA +35% (~$450-550k/year) Additional staff, district offices, constituent services Public Matching 6:1 up to $5M Neutralizes wealthy self-funded challengers

docs.title: Career Value Impact
docs.text:
Conservative estimate for 10-year House career under CMA vs. current system: approximately +$3M in total compensation value (salary increases, enhanced pension, professional development, MRA capacity).

This is not the reason to support CMA. But it is relevant to understanding what the legislation provides.

Prepared by: Albert E. Ramos, Director The American Policy Architecture Institute

Contact: info@policyarchitecture.org Website:

docs.title: Legislative Overview
docs.text:
Published January 2026

A comprehensive description of the Congressional Modernization Act's structure, provisions, and implementation timeline

docs.title: Purpose of This Document
docs.text:
This overview provides a systematic walk-through of the Congressional Modernization Act, describing what the legislation does without advocacy or defense. It serves as a reference guide for understanding the Act's comprehensive reform architecture across eight integrated titles.

The CMA addresses what might be called the "maintenance and modernization needs" of American representative democracy -- updating electoral infrastructure, expanding institutional capacity, and aligning incentive structures to enable effective 21st-century governance.

docs.title: I. STRUCTURAL OVERVIEW
docs.text:
The Congressional Modernization Act consists of eight titles that work as an integrated system:

Title I: Federal Ballot Access Standards establishes uniform requirements for candidates seeking ballot access in federal elections, replacing fifty different state regimes with clear federal standards.

Title II: Federal Political Party Recognition creates a federal framework for recognizing political parties and implementing Joint Endorsement Lists, allowing multiple parties to endorse the same candidate on federal ballots.

Title III: House Membership Expansion expands the House of Representatives from 435 to approximately 720 members over approximately 14 years through an initial expansion followed by biennial increases, improving representation ratios and institutional capacity.

Title IV: Multi-Member Districts and STAR-PR transitions congressional districts from single-member to multi-member configuration using proportional representation, eliminating the mathematical distortions inherent in winner-take-all systems.

Title V: Non-Qualifying Federal Office Examinations establishes the Federal Candidate Assessment Office (FCAO) to administer standardized competency examinations for all federal candidates, with results displayed on ballots for voter information.

Title VI: Transition, Compensation, and Implementation provides immediate compensation and capacity improvements for current members while establishing transition protections that phase in as electoral reforms are implemented.

Title VII: Enforcement, Reporting, and Oversight establishes enforcement mechanisms, reporting requirements, judicial review procedures, and transition support infrastructure.

Title VIII: General Provisions addresses severability, constitutional supremacy, effective dates, and technical definitions including the critical "First CMA Election" and "Second CMA Election" triggers.

The legislation addresses a fundamental political economy problem: electoral reforms typically require incumbents to vote away their own advantages. The CMA resolves this by providing substantial immediate benefits to all current members upon enactment while launching integrated electoral reforms at the First CMA Election.

docs.title: II. IMPLEMENTATION TIMELINE
docs.text: The CMA's reforms implement through a tiered effective date structure designed to provide immediate member benefits, ensure simultaneous integrated launch of electoral reforms, and allow adequate development time for FCAO examinations.

docs.title: Tier 1: Immediate upon Enactment
docs.text:
Title VI (Compensation and Capacity): All compensation provisions take effect immediately upon enactment, including phased salary increases, enhanced pension formula, professional development allowances, and streamlined Senate appointment authority.

Title VII (Enforcement): FEC and DOJ receive immediate authority to begin rulemaking, issue guidance, and prepare for implementation.

Title VIII (General Provisions): Definitions, severability, and administrative authorities become operative from Day One to provide legal scaffolding.

docs.title: Tier 2: First CMA Election
docs.text:
The "First CMA Election" is defined as the second regularly scheduled general election for Congress occurring after enactment, except that if such second election occurs less than eighteen (18) months after enactment, the First CMA Election shall instead be the third regularly scheduled general election after enactment. This ensures a minimum 18-month runway for state preparation.

At the First CMA Election, the following provisions take effect simultaneously:

Title I (Ballot Access): Federal ballot access standards become operative for all federal elections.

Title II (Party Recognition): Federal party recognition framework and Joint Endorsement Lists take effect.

Title III (House Expansion): House expands to 510 members. Biennial +35 seat increases begin with subsequent Congresses.

Title IV (Multi-Member Districts): Multi-member districts begin phasing in at 25% (Transition Cycle 1). STAR voting implemented for all federal elections.

docs.title: Tier 3: Second CMA Election
docs.text:
The "Second CMA Election" is defined as the first regularly scheduled general election for Congress occurring after the First CMA Election.

Title V (FCAO): Federal Candidate Assessment Office examinations become mandatory. FCAO scores appear on ballots for first time. The decoupled timeline provides 36-42 months for proper exam development, pilot testing, and validation.

docs.title: Ongoing: Biennial Increases
docs.text: Beginning with the second Congress elected after initial expansion, House membership increases by 35 seats each Congress until reaching the cube root of the national population (approximately 720 members). This achieves cube root compliance within approximately 14 years -- three presidential terms.

docs.title: Timeline Summary
docs.text:
Trigger Provisions Approximate Timing Enactment Titles VI, VII, VIII Day One First CMA Election Titles I, II, III, IV ~2-3 years after enactment Second CMA Election Title V ~4-5 years after enactment Biennial increases Title III ongoing Every 2 years until cube root Cube root compliance Title III terminates ~14 years after enactment

This structure ensures members receive immediate benefits while electoral reforms launch together as an integrated system, producing "the new Congress" rather than "more of the old Congress."

docs.title: III. TITLE I: FEDERAL BALLOT ACCESS STANDARDS
docs.text: Title I establishes uniform federal requirements for ballot access in federal elections, exercising Congress's clear authority under the Elections Clause (Article I, Section 4, Clause 1). Currently, fifty different state ballot access regimes create varying signature requirements from 100 to over 100,000, inconsistent filing deadlines, different petition formats, and substantial litigation exposure.

docs.title: Three Paths to Ballot Access
docs.text:
The Act creates three alternative pathways for candidates to achieve ballot access:

Major Party Nomination: Candidates nominated by federally recognized political parties whose candidate for the same office received at least 5% of votes in the most recent election automatically qualify for ballot access.

Signature Petition: Candidates may qualify by collecting petition signatures equal to 0.5% of votes cast for that office in the most recent election. The Act caps requirements at 5,000 signatures for statewide offices and 1,000 signatures for House districts. States must provide at least 120 days for signature collection and must accept signatures from any registered voter in the jurisdiction regardless of party affiliation. Electronic signature collection through FEC-approved secure methods is permitted.

Filing Fee Alternative: Candidates may pay a filing fee not exceeding $1,000 for House seats or $2,500 for statewide offices, with amounts adjusted annually for inflation. This provides an efficiency path for candidates with resources while keeping fees modest enough to avoid wealth barriers.

docs.title: Additional Requirements
docs.text:
States must establish filing deadlines at least 120 days before elections to provide adequate time for signature collection or paperwork filing. Signature verification must occur within 30 days of submission, with preliminary counts provided within 15 days. Candidates may submit supplemental signatures to cure deficiencies.

States must treat independent and minor party candidates identically to major party candidates regarding ballot position (determined by random selection or rotation), ballot formatting, access to voter lists, participation in voter guides and candidate forums, and all other election administration aspects.

Write-in candidates are permitted for all federal offices provided they file a declaration of candidacy at least 30 days before the election.

docs.title: Enforcement and Effective Date
docs.text:
Any candidate denied ballot access in violation of Title I may bring civil action in federal district court, with expedited review given the time-sensitive nature of ballot access disputes. The Federal Election Commission receives enforcement authority and must promulgate implementing regulations within 180 days of enactment.

Title I takes effect at the First CMA Election, launching simultaneously with party recognition, House expansion, and multi-member districts as part of the integrated reform package.

docs.title: IV. TITLE II: FEDERAL POLITICAL PARTY RECOGNITION
docs.text: Title II establishes federal standards for recognizing political parties and implements Joint Endorsement Lists, allowing multiple parties to formally endorse the same candidate on federal ballots. This system differs from traditional fusion voting where candidates appear multiple times on ballots with vote aggregation -- under Joint Endorsement Lists, candidates appear once with up to three party endorsements displayed.

docs.title: Party Recognition Standards
docs.text:
To qualify as a federally recognized political party, organizations must meet one of two criteria:

Electoral Performance Standard: The party's candidate for President, Senate, or House received at least 5% of votes in the most recent federal election in at least 15 states.

Membership Standard: The party demonstrates at least 100,000 registered members nationally, members in at least 25 states, no single state comprising more than 25% of total membership, formal organizational structure including bylaws and elected leadership, democratic internal decision-making processes, and basic financial transparency with annual FEC reporting.

Recognized parties maintain status by meeting either standard in each four-year cycle, filing annual reports documenting organizational structure and finances, maintaining democratic endorsement procedures, and endorsing at least one federal candidate every four years.

docs.title: Joint Endorsement Lists
docs.text:
Federally recognized parties may endorse candidates who accept their endorsement. Endorsed candidates appear on ballots with party designations clearly displayed. Candidates may receive up to three party endorsements, displayed in order chosen by the candidate.

Ballot format shows: "Candidate Name (Party A / Party B / Party C)" or equivalent clear display of multiple endorsements.

Parties retain full autonomy in their endorsement processes, which may include conventions, committee decisions, primaries, or other methods consistent with party bylaws. Crucially, parties may endorse candidates who also receive endorsements from other parties -- no party may prohibit endorsed candidates from accepting additional endorsements.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The FEC must promulgate implementing regulations within 180 days of enactment, covering party registration procedures, membership verification standards, electoral performance calculation methods, ballot display technical standards, and enforcement procedures.

Title II takes effect at the First CMA Election, launching simultaneously with ballot access standardization, House expansion, and multi-member districts.

docs.title: V. TITLE III: HOUSE MEMBERSHIP EXPANSION
docs.text: Title III expands House membership from 435 to approximately 720 members over approximately 14 years, addressing the representation crisis created by population growth without corresponding House expansion since 1913. Current representation ratio stands at approximately 760,000 constituents per Representative -- six to seven times larger than peer democracies and compared to the founding era's ratio around 30,000:1.

docs.title: Accelerated Expansion Structure
docs.text:
Initial Expansion (First CMA Election): House expands to 510 members (+75 seats), improving representation ratio to approximately 657,000:1. This initial expansion launches simultaneously with STAR voting and multi-member districts, ensuring the new seats are filled through the new electoral system rather than replicated dysfunction.

Biennial Increases: Beginning with the second Congress elected after initial expansion, House membership increases by 35 seats each Congress. This incremental approach sounds like routine institutional housekeeping rather than revolutionary transformation.

Termination at Cube Root: Biennial increases continue until House size equals or exceeds the cube root of the resident population of the United States as determined by the most recent decennial census. Based on current population projections, this occurs at approximately 720 members, roughly 14 years after enactment.

Post-Stabilization Adjustment: After biennial increases terminate, House size adjusts only once per decennial census, sized at the integer nearest to the cube root of the resident population.

docs.title: Apportionment and Infrastructure
docs.text:
The Clerk of the House uses population data from the most recently completed decennial census to calculate House size according to the applicable formula, apportions seats among states using the method of equal proportions, and announces apportionment at least 12 months before each expansion. Each state receives at least one Representative as constitutionally required.

House expansion timing operates independently of decennial census timing. Apportionment always uses the most recent census data available, but expansions occur on the schedule established in Title III regardless of when censuses are conducted.

The Architect of the Capitol must develop plans for House Chamber expansion, additional office space, and technology infrastructure within 12 months of enactment. Congress appropriates funds for infrastructure improvements to be completed at least 12 months before initial expansion. GAO and CBO jointly prepare comprehensive reports analyzing facility requirements, technology needs, staff expansion costs, and budget impacts within 180 days of enactment.

docs.title: Enhanced Resources
docs.text:
Upon initial House expansion, each Representative's Member Representational Allowance (MRA) increases by 35% above pre-expansion levels, adjusted annually for inflation. MRA amounts receive review and adjustment at subsequent expansion milestones to ensure adequate resources relative to district size.

States redistrict following each expansion to accommodate increased representation, using the most recent census data and applying Title IV standards for multi-member districts where applicable.

docs.title: VI. TITLE IV: MULTI-MEMBER DISTRICTS AND STAR-PR
docs.text: Title IV transitions congressional districts from single-member to multi-member configuration using proportional representation to eliminate geometric distortions inherent in winner-take-all systems. Single-member districts combined with plurality voting create mathematical distortions regardless of who draws the lines: 51% of votes yields 100% of representation in that district, like-minded voters clustering creates outcomes determined by boundaries rather than preferences, and redistricting disputes arise every census cycle.

docs.title: Historical and Constitutional Context
docs.text: Multi-member congressional districts were standard American practice from 1789 through 1967. The 1967 federal mandate for single-member districts was statutory choice, not constitutional requirement. The Supreme Court explicitly validated multi-member districts in Whitcomb v. Chavis (1971) and White v. Regester (1973). Title IV reinstates historical practice while adding modern proportional allocation methods.

docs.title: Gradual Phase-In
docs.text:
Multi-member districts implement over five election cycles beginning with Transition Cycle 1 (the First CMA Election):

  • Transition Cycle 1 (First CMA Election): States may elect up to 25% of Representatives from multi-member districts
  • Transition Cycle 2: Up to 50%
  • Transition Cycle 3: Up to 75%
  • Transition Cycle 4: Up to 90%
  • Full Implementation (Fifth election following Transition Cycle 1): 100% of Representatives elected from multi-member districts

During Transition Cycles 1-4, incumbent Representatives may choose to remain in current single-member districts if they continue to exist or compete in newly created multi-member districts. No incumbent faces mandatory participation in multi-member districts until Full Implementation.

docs.title: District Requirements
docs.text:
Multi-member congressional districts must elect between 3 and 7 Representatives per district, contain substantially equal population across districts within each state, comply with Voting Rights Act and one-person-one-vote requirements, maintain geographic contiguity, and respect county and municipal boundaries where practicable. Districts may not be drawn to favor or disfavor any political party or incumbent.

States with fewer than three Representatives total are exempt from multi-member requirements and continue using single-winner STAR voting.

docs.title: STAR Voting Method
docs.text:
All federal elections use STAR voting (Score Then Automatic Runoff): voters score each candidate from 0 to 5 stars, the system maximizes voter satisfaction while maintaining accountability, and the familiar interface eliminates spoiler effects while reducing strategic voting incentives.

For single-winner contests (President, Senate, House districts electing fewer than three Representatives, and primaries in single-member districts): voters score all candidates 0-5 stars, the two highest-scoring candidates advance to an automatic runoff, and the candidate preferred by more voters in the runoff wins.

For multi-member House districts electing three or more Representatives, STAR-PR (Score Then Automatic Runoff - Proportional Representation, also known as Allocated Score) is used: voters score all candidates 0-5 stars, total scores are calculated for each candidate, seats are allocated proportionally using quota calculations and sequential proportional approval logic to maximize voter satisfaction while ensuring proportionality. The system ensures that vote share translates directly to seat share within districts -- in a 5-seat district where Party A receives 60% support and Party B receives 40% support, the result approximates 3 seats for Party A and 2 seats for Party B.

docs.title: Unified Election Structure
docs.text:
The Act establishes single general elections as the universal standard for all federal offices:

All Federal Elections: Qualified candidates meeting Title I ballot access requirements appear directly on the general election ballot with party endorsements (Title II) and FCAO scores (Title V) displayed. States do not conduct primary elections for federal offices.

Rationale: STAR voting's scoring mechanism ensures winners have both high overall support and majority preference, eliminating the traditional justification for preliminary filtering through primaries. Single general elections maximize voter participation, eliminate party registration barriers, reduce administrative costs, and ensure consistency of voter experience regardless of state of residence.

Party Autonomy: Political parties retain full authority to conduct internal nomination processes -- caucuses, conventions, advisory polls -- at their own expense and independent of state election infrastructure. Joint Endorsement Lists provide a streamlined mechanism for parties to signal preferred candidates to voters.

docs.title: Implementation Support
docs.text:
The FEC must publish detailed technical specifications for STAR voting and STAR-PR implementation within 12 months of enactment, covering ballot design standards, vote tabulation procedures, audit protocols, equipment certification requirements, and recount procedures.
